he   istis   ophia tet which forms the contents of the skew
ode was made available to scholars over a century ago. owever
a modern nglish translation of this optic gnostic document has
been lacking for some years, and it is advantageous that previously
known gnostic writings should now be included in the ag am-
madi tudies eries. he admirable edition and erman transla-
tion by . chmidt, re-edited by . ill   (ibi.    and ) would
be hard to supersede for this reason a new edit'ion of the optic
tet   is considered unnecessary. he present nglish translation
is therefore based on the optic tet as edited and emended by
chmidt (ibi. ), and the optic tet is reproduced from this
edition unaltered ecept for minor corrections. or purposes of
study and comparison with the erman, the generat format of
ibi.  has been retained in   the   nglish translation.
or easy reference to previous translations, all the indees are
based on the page numbers of chmidt's edition of the optic
tet (ibi. ) which are retained   in the present volume,   both
for the tet and the translation. n the erman translation of the
 istis   ophia (ibi. ), these page numbers appear in the right-
hand margin. ivision of the tet into paragraphs and numbered
chapters corresponds to that in ibi. . he verses of psalms
are likewise numbered.
ny new translation has to be made in the light of those already
in eistence, and this one owes much   to the work of chmidt
and ill. n important earlier edition and translation was that
of chwartze and   etermann   (ibi.   ), to which there are frequent
references in chmidt's tetual notes. n the past there have been
a   number of occasions   where   opinions   among   scholars   have
differed on the correct rendering of various passages in this tet.
lthough in the main the present translation closely follows that
of chmidt and ill, there are some minor differences. n such
cases, the alternative readings or versions are given as footnotes.
ill's notes on chmidt's translation, which appear in the ppen-
di to ibi. , are also incorporated into the present footnotes.

chmidt's footnotes to the optic tet have been checked with
the manuscript, and are given   here in   nglish translation.   is
references to chwartze's readings have been omitted.
he use of modern nglish poses certain problems in trans-
lating the istis ophia. he tet contains numerous quotations
from and allusions to the   ld   and   ew estaments which,   to
nglish-speaking readers, are probably familiar in the language
ofthe evised ersion. lt seemed best to adopt a somewhat formal
nglish prose style, and this, it is hoped, will be both suitable and
not unduly archaic.   lt   will be seen that words of reek origin
are italicised. he alternative of giving the reek words in brackets
after the nglish words would have been very repetitive and would
have added considerably to the cost of publication.  key to these
words of reek derivation is given on page . ertain reek
words, some of which are gnostic technical terms, have been left
untranslated. ollowing chmidt and ill, it was thought prefer-
able to give words of uncertain meaning in transliteration, rather
than to attempt a translation which might be misleading.   t   has
been possible in some cases to indicate in the footnotes a compar-
able use of such words in the ag ammadi tets and elsewhere.
he   ibliography   represents   a   selection   from   what   is   now
a considerable accumulation of literature. arly writings of his-
toric interest or likely to be of relevance today are included. or
others not mentioned here, the bibliographies in . . . ead
(ibi. ), . offatt (ibi. ) and . ill (ibi. ) should be
consulted. eview articles on modern writers are listed in . .
choler   ag ammadi ibliography.
 y   acknowledgements   are due   to   yldendal   ublishers for
permission to reproduce the optic tet of istis ophia also to
the rustees of the ritish useum and their successors in the
ritish ibrary for facilities to study the manuscript in the epart-
ment of riental anuscripts and rinted ooks.  am grateful
to r.   . .   dridge, ssistant anager of . . rill, for his
care and attention in the preparation of this volume. y thanks
are also due to the ditorial oard of the ag ammadi tudies
for the opportunity to contribute to their series in particular to
my   volume   editor,   rofessor   .   c.   ilson,   who   has   read
through the translation and made many helpful suggestions. ny

remaining   errors   are   my   responsibility.   o   rofessor   artin
rause, for bis kind encouragement over many years,    would
also like to epress my gratitude.

istory of the skew ode
 be skew ode, a volume of unknown provenance containing
tbe tets of tbe istis   opbia   treatises, was named after its first
owner, . skew, a ondon doctor. skew was a collector of
old manuscripts,   and   be   bougbt tbe code from   a   bookseller
(probably in ondon) in   .      fter tbe deatb of skew tbe
manuscript was bougbt by tbe ritisb useum.  copy in   tbe
ritisb useum of tbe sale catalogue () of skew's manu-
scripts oontains tbe entry    "optic   .,   f.   .   .   ."   bis reference
was presumed by rum to apply to tbe present document wbicb
appears in bis catalogue as  ll. 
. . oide, to wbom skew gave tbe task of studying tbe
manuscript, first brougbt it to the attention of tbeological readers, 
introducing it under tbe title   "istis   opbia"   wbicb it bas since
retained. oide also quoted the ew estament citations in bis

five   "des   of   olomon"   which were published by the   anisb

was made   by   .   ularier between    and   ,   again   with
the intention tbat it sbould be published.   owever the manu-
script of ularier never appeared   in   print and   is   now   in   the
 ibliotbeque   ationale in aris   (ibi.   ).

 (ibi.   ). his set out to be a literal translation, even keeping
to the optic word order.
he translation of the tet which has stood the test of time
was published   in ermany by .   chmidt   in      (ibi.   ).
e was given the task of editing and translating the then known
optic gnostic tets by the irchenväter-ommission (now the
ommission   für   spätantike   eligionsgeschichte)   of the   erlin
kademie der issenschaften.   he   volume   contained   a   trans-
 lation of the istis   ophia,   followed by transations of the optic
gnostic tets contained in the   ruce ode. hese translations
were fully annotated and preceded by an introductory discussion.
 second edition of chmidt's translation of the istis   ophia
 (ibi.   ) and bis annotated edition of the optic tet   (ibi.   )
appeared   in   .   ince   this date   there   have   been   no   further
editions of the tet. n  . ill published a new edition of
chmidt's (      and   ) translations   (ibi.   ).   e followed
chmidt's versions closely, giving his own alternative renderings
of certain passages in an appendi.  third edition, revised   by
ill, appeared in , and a reprint of the third edition in .
 escription of the anuscript
 he skew ode is described in . . rum's   atalogue o.f
optic anrtscripts in   the   ritish useum   under the heading of
 ahidic   anuscripts.
he manuscript, which now consists of  leaves in a modern

binding, originally comprised  eaves of parchment. he leaves
measure         . cms. he tet   is written   in   two columns on
both sides of the leaves ( sides), and is the work   of   two scribes.
he first scribe numbered the pages on the rectos only, the second
on rectos and versos. he book consists of  quires, the   tirst
of which   consists of    leaves,   the   last   of    leaves,   and   the
remainder each of  leaves. our leaves from the inside of one
quire are missing, so that there is a lacuna between pages 
and . he tirst two blank and unnumbered pages and the last
four are also missing. he state of preservation of most of the
tet is very good.   n   parts of some pages the ink has faded, and
in these places the script is only just legible.
he tet consists of four   "books".   ook   ne   ends with   the
tirst column on p.    (chmidt ) and has no title, either at
the beginning or end. he second column of p.   , which was
left blank, was later tilled with tet by another hand.
ook wo begins on p. .  title at the head of this page 
 he   econd   ook of the istis   ophia   is a later addition. he
whole tet is now known as the   istis   ophia   on the basis of this
insertion.  title of the second book occurs on p.  (chmidt
.) at the foot of the tirst column     art of the ooks of the
 aviour.   he tet continues in the second column, and ends in
the second column of p.  (chmidt .). he last sentence
of p.  suggests that this is the end of the book, rather than
p.   .   lt   is suggested   by chmidt and   ill   that   because   the
contents of the tirst and second columns of p.    appear to
follow one another, the title properly belongs at the end of the
tet on p. .
ook hree thus begins on   p.    (chmidt ), and ends
on p.  (chmidt ).  title similar to that of ook wo
stands below the last line of the first column on p. .
ook our begins in the second column of p.  (chmidt )
and ends in the second column of p.  (chmidt ).  para-
graph in a later hand stands at the head of the first column of
p.  (chmidt ).
he end of the istis ophia account proper occurs in ook
wo (chmidt p. . hapter ). hereafter the tet consists
of teachings given to the disciples in the form of answers to their

questions.   t   may be noted here that there is a considerable ack
of homogeneity in composition. ven within the individual books
the narrative appears to rely upon a number of documents placed
in approimate sequence.   s the various   "documents"   contain
different portions of the account, this gives rise, on the one hand,
to repetitions -   some episodes are described more than once -
and on the other, to anomalies   in   the names of the speakers.
hus the central figure in ook   ne   is named as esus in ook
wo, as the irst ystery, then as esus and at the end, as the
aviour in ook   ,   as esus and the. aviour alternately and
in ook , as esus. ary agdalene appears as   aria and
ariam (ariham) in different parts of the tet. hese incon-
sistencies support the view that   the   tet   is   a   compilation.   ts
authorship, date, provenance and purpose cannot be discussed
here.  guide to the ayout of the four   books   is given below,
followed by a brief summary of the contents.
